summary:
The Battle of Chaldiran (August 23, 1514 AD) was the opening engagement of the [[event:2513|Safavid-Ottoman Wars]] — a decisive confrontation in eastern Anatolia in which the Ottoman Sultan Selim I's forces crushed the Safavid Shah Ismail I's army, ending Ismail's aura of invincibility (which his Qizilbash followers had believed made him divinely protected) and determining that the Ottomans rather than Safavids would dominate Anatolia and Mesopotamia. The battle turned primarily on Ottoman possession of artillery and firearms: the Safavid cavalry, however brave, could not withstand concentrated cannon and janissary musket fire, and Ismail was wounded on the battlefield — his first military defeat after 14 years of unbroken victories.

Chaldiran's consequences were profound for both the Safavid state and for Ismail personally. Selim occupied Tabriz, the Safavid capital, briefly, demonstrating that Persia's heartland was vulnerable. Shah Ismail never personally led an army in the field again after Chaldiran — the defeat shattered his sense of divine mission. For the Safavid state, Chaldiran was a harsh lesson in the importance of military technology: subsequent Safavid rulers invested heavily in firearms and artillery, ultimately modernizing the Persian military. The battle also confirmed the religious stakes of the conflict: Selim had executed tens of thousands of Anatolian Shia Muslims before the battle, regarding them as potential Safavid collaborators — an act that further hardened the sectarian divide between Ottoman and Safavid spheres.
